# Patient Details: Timothy Parker

## Patient Information
- Name: Timothy Parker
- DOB: 2012-06-09
- Sex: Male
- Member ID: CT151905981
- Address: 5310 Court St, Norwich, CT 06900

## Subscriber Information
- Name: Stephen Parker
- DOB: 1981-08-23
- Relation: Other

## Provider Information
- Name: James Rodriguez
- NPI: 6735405004
- Phone: (475) 357-9962
- Fax: (203) 206-6513
- Address: 3878 Maple St, Windsor, CT 06868

## Lab Information
- Name: LabCorp
- NPI: 2063391976
- Address: 6790 Church St, Torrington, CT 06147

## Test Information
- Test Type: WGS
- Configuration: Trio
- Urgency: Expedited
- Specimen: Buccal
- Collection Date: 2026-01-09

## Clinical Information
Timothy Parker is a 13-year-old male with history notable for a disorder of phenylalanine metabolism and recurrent episodes of seizure activity. Records describe persistent hyperphenylalaninemia on monitoring, with plasma phenylalanine typically in the 900–1200 µmol/L range on several checks over the past year (most recently 1050 µmol/L), with comparatively normal tyrosine around 55–75 µmol/L, consistent with ongoing elevation rather than a single episodic spike. Clinically, he has had intermittent seizure-like events characterized by sudden loss of awareness with generalized stiffening and rhythmic shaking, lasting approximately 1–2 minutes, followed by post-event sleepiness and confusion for 20–30 minutes; episodes have occurred a few times per year and have resulted in missed school days after events. Family history is significant for a paternal uncle with a childhood-onset seizure disorder and a maternal cousin reported to have elevated phenylalanine on newborn screening requiring ongoing dietary management.

# Patient Information
Name: Pamela Morgan
DOB: 2013-09-05
Sex: Female
Member ID: HUS959703481
Address: 4982 Oak Ave, Norwalk, CT 06314

# Subscriber Information
Name: Mary Morgan
DOB: 1983-01-05
Relation: Guardian

# Provider Information
Name: Joseph Harris
NPI: 5749751326
Phone: (475) 582-5499
Fax: (475) 726-3131
Address: 9336 Mill St, Norwich, CT 06442

# Lab Information
Name: LabCorp
NPI: 6750633150
Address: 9626 Maple St, Bridgeport, CT 06624

# Test Information
Test Type: WES
Configuration: Proband
Urgency: Regular
Specimen: Saliva

# Clinical Information
Pamela Morgan is a 12-year-old female with a history notable for congenital brain malformation consistent with holoprosencephaly, accompanied by dysmorphic features including microtia of the external ear and subtle midline facial differences. She has a documented congenital esophageal atresia, with early feeding and swallowing difficulties related to the structural interruption of the esophagus; current history continues to reference the congenital condition when reviewing growth and nutrition. Neurodevelopmental history is significant for a specific developmental disorder of motor function, with longstanding impairment in coordination and fine-motor control that affects daily tasks such as handwriting, dressing skills, and age-expected gross motor activities. The overall phenotype has been described as a congenital malformation syndrome predominantly affecting facial appearance, and clinical documentation emphasizes the co-occurrence of craniofacial and central nervous system findings rather than a single isolated anomaly. Prior genetic evaluation included a gene panel performed on 2025-11-15, reported as negative.

Clinical Information: Adam Scott is a 13-year-old male with history notable for recurrent seizures of unspecified type, described by caregivers as intermittent episodes of sudden unresponsiveness with generalized stiffening and rhythmic shaking lasting 1–2 minutes, followed by confusion and fatigue for up to an hour, occurring a few times per year without a consistent trigger. He also carries a history of acidosis; available laboratory data show persistently low serum bicarbonate around 16–18 mmol/L with a mildly elevated anion gap (approximately 18–20) on repeat checks, with episodes of worsening fatigue and tachypnea during intercurrent illness when bicarbonate has fallen to ~14 mmol/L. Prior genetic testing includes chromosomal microarray performed on 2025-10-09 with a negative result.

I have completed approximately 80% of the Pre-Authorization Form for Jacob Hernandez (Member ID: HUS254207351), including the Patient, Subscriber, Provider, Lab, and Test Requested sections. However, I have encountered a blocker on the 'Diagnoses & Clinical Information' section. 

Issue: The form requires specific ICD-10 codes and descriptions. While the EHR record for Jacob Hernandez provides detailed clinical descriptions (Congenital hepatic fibrosis, Polycystic kidney disease, Craniosynostosis, Congenital exophthalmos, Speech and language delay, and Midface hypoplasia), it does not list the corresponding ICD-10 numeric codes. Per the instructions to 'stop the process and report the issue' if any issues are found, I am terminating the task now. All other required information has been successfully gathered and entered into the preceding form sections.

- Address: 7432 Park Rd, Stamford, CT 06011

## Lab Information
- Name: GeneDx
- NPI: 8726793483
- Address: 9048 Main St, Stratford, CT 06216

## Test Information
- Test Type: WGS
- Configuration: Duo
- Urgency: Expedited
- Specimen: Buccal

## Clinical Information
Sarah Wright is a 9-year-old female with history notable for a disorder of aromatic amino-acid metabolism consistent with tyrosinemia and episodic seizure-like events. Metabolic monitoring has shown persistently elevated plasma tyrosine, typically in the 520–680 µmol/L range (reference 35–110), with intermittent increases during intercurrent illness; phenylalanine has remained within reference range, and liver-associated enzymes have not shown a consistent pattern of elevation in available records. Caregivers report dietary sensitivity in which higher natural protein intake correlates with worsening irritability and reduced appetite, though growth has been maintained. Neurologically, she has had recurrent paroxysmal events described as sudden stiffening with unresponsiveness lasting about 1–2 minutes, followed by post-event sleepiness; frequency has varied from rare single episodes to clusters over several weeks, without a clearly identified trigger, and events have impacted school attendance due to fatigue afterward. Family history is significant for a paternal cousin with childhood-onset seizures and a maternal uncle with a documented tyrosine metabolism disorder; parents deny consanguinity.

# Patient Details: Nicholas Davis

## Patient Information
- Name: Nicholas Davis
- DOB: 2021-09-01
- Sex: Male
- Member ID: 462016309 (Full: CT462016309)
- Address: 4178 Maple St, Meriden, CT 06887

## Subscriber Information
- Name: David Davis
- DOB: 1996-10-25
- Relation: Parent

## Provider Information
- Name: Thomas Taylor
- NPI: 7826908164
- Phone: (475) 750-3994
- Fax: (475) 769-5623
- Address: 6978 Spring St, Danbury, CT 06563

## Lab Information
- Name: LabCorp
- NPI: 9166077634
- Address: 1778 Pleasant St, Manchester, CT 06104

## Test Information
- Test Type: WGS
- Configuration: Proband
- Urgency: Expedited
- Specimen: Saliva
- Collection Date: N/A

## Clinical Information
Nicholas Davis is a 4-year-old male with a history notable for a mitochondrial metabolism disorder and epilepsy characterized by generalized seizures without status epilepticus. Caregivers describe recurrent convulsive events with abrupt loss of awareness and generalized stiffening and jerking, followed by postictal sleepiness, with episodes occurring intermittently rather than in a continuous or prolonged cluster. Between events, he returns to baseline but has required ongoing neurologic follow-up for seizure control. Metabolic evaluation has been consistent with a mitochondrial disorder, including persistently elevated serum lactate around 3.8–4.6 mmol/L (ref 0.5–2.2) with an increased lactate-to-pyruvate ratio of approximately 28, and episodic worsening during intercurrent illness with lactate reported up to 6.1 mmol/L. Family history is notable for seizures in a maternal uncle beginning in childhood and a paternal cousin with a diagnosed mitochondrial metabolism disorder; parents deny consanguinity.

Kevin Collins is a 14-year-old male with a history notable for severe intellectual disability with significant limitations in adaptive functioning; he requires close supervision for daily activities and has longstanding global developmental impairment consistent with a multiple congenital anomalies picture. Dysmorphic features are present, including facial asymmetry and low-set ears. Overall presentation is consistent with a defined congenital malformation syndrome involving multiple systems. # Patient Details: Bennett Claremont

## Patient Information
- Name: Bennett Claremont
- DOB: 2012-10-28
- Sex: Male
- Member ID: CT439804479
- Address: 6370 Pine St, New Haven, CT 06715

## Subscriber Information
- Name: Nadia Claremont
- DOB: 1979-02-11
- Relation: Guardian

## Provider Information
- Name: Emily Davis
- NPI: 7977379419
- Phone: (860) 523-8860
- Fax: (203) 260-8195
- Address: 5136 State St, Bristol, CT 06145

## Lab Information
- Name: LabCorp
- NPI: 4437940858
- Address: 6132 School St, New Haven, CT 06505

## Test Information
- Test Type: WES
- Configuration: Trio
- Urgency: Regular
- Specimen: Blood
- Collection Date: 2026-02-04

## Clinical Information
Bennett Claremont is a 13-year-old male with history notable for neonatal hypotonia, described as “floppy” in early life with reduced spontaneous antigravity movements and weak suck requiring feeding support during the newborn period. Tone gradually improved over time, though the family reports he has remained less robust with endurance than peers. Metabolic evaluation has been pursued for persistent, nonspecific abnormal blood chemistry findings; available records describe recurring mild elevations in plasma lactate (typically 2.8–3.6 mmol/L, ref <2.0) with intermittent mild transaminase elevation (ALT 65–90 U/L) on repeat sampling, without a single unifying named metabolic diagnosis documented in the chart. These laboratory findings have been noted on more than one occasion rather than as an isolated event. Family history is significant for a maternal uncle who reportedly had marked neonatal “floppiness” and feeding difficulty, and a paternal first cousin with similar early hypotonia requiring early therapy; there is no reported parental consanguinity.

# Test Information
Type: WES
Configuration: Duo
Urgency: Expedited
Specimen: Buccal
Collection Date: 2026-01-27

# Clinical Information
Kieran Griffith is a 5-year-old male with history notable for congenital lactase deficiency with dietary sensitivity since infancy, characterized by reproducible watery diarrhea, abdominal cramping, and excessive gas after lactose-containing feeds. Symptoms are persistent when lactose is ingested and improve with strict lactose avoidance; family reports careful label reading and use of lactose-free alternatives, with intermittent breakthrough episodes after accidental exposures. Prior metabolic evaluation has shown repeatedly low lactase activity on stool enzyme testing, and during symptomatic periods he has had mild non–anion gap metabolic acidosis (serum bicarbonate 18–20 mmol/L) and dehydration markers that resolve with rehydration and dietary restriction. Neurologic history includes episodic seizures without a specified type, described as sudden loss of awareness with generalized stiffening and rhythmic jerking lasting ~1–2 minutes, followed by postictal sleepiness; frequency is variable with months between events, and there is no consistent trigger reported. Family history is significant for similar lactose intolerance symptoms in the father and paternal grandmother, and a maternal uncle with recurrent seizures. Parents are first cousins.

- Test Type: WES

- Urgency: Expedited
- Specimen: Buccal
- Collection Date: 2026-01-27

## Clinical Information
Brady Hadley is a 6-year-old male with history notable for epilepsy characterized by generalized seizures that have occurred without clear focal onset; caregivers describe intermittent convulsive episodes with postictal sleepiness and transient confusion, impacting school attendance and requiring close supervision during activities such as bathing and swimming. In addition, prior evaluations have documented abnormal blood chemistry findings. Available laboratory data include persistently elevated serum lactate in the range of 3.8–4.6 mmol/L (reference 0.5–2.2) on repeat measurements, with episodic worsening during intercurrent illness, and mildly increased plasma alanine (e.g., 610 µmol/L, reference 200–500), prompting continued monitoring for a metabolic contribution to symptoms without assigning a specific metabolic diagnosis in the record. Family history is significant for seizures in a maternal uncle beginning in adolescence and a paternal first cousin with recurrent generalized convulsions; parents deny known consanguinity. No other prior test types, dates, or results were provided in the chart for review.

## Test Information
- Test Type: WES
- Configuration: Duo
- Urgency: Regular
- Specimen: Buccal
- Collection Date: 2026-01-14

## Clinical Information
Arlo Valentine is a 7-year-old male with a history notable for congenital anomalies and developmental coordination difficulties. Craniofacial findings include a congenital malformation of the ear with visible external ear asymmetry, along with positional plagiocephaly characterized by occipital flattening and mild facial asymmetry on inspection. He also carries a diagnosis consistent with a defined congenital malformation syndrome involving multiple congenital anomalies, and his overall phenotype includes both ear differences and cranial shape asymmetry as described above. Renal history is notable for congenital medullary sponge kidney, with prior clinical documentation of nephrocalcinosis risk and episodic hematuria prompting renal imaging consistent with this diagnosis. Neurodevelopmentally, he has developmental coordination disorder with persistent fine- and gross-motor coordination challenges that affect age-appropriate tasks such as handwriting, dressing skills, and playground activities. Prior genetic testing included a single-gene test performed on 2026-01-20, reported as negative.

Clinical Information: Griffin Alder is an 11-year-old male with history notable for impaired coordination consistent with ataxia, described as a persistent unsteady gait with frequent stumbling and difficulty with balance-dependent tasks such as running, climbing stairs without holding a rail, and participating in sports; symptoms are observed across settings and have functional impact at school and during play. Laboratory evaluation has also shown other specified abnormal findings of blood chemistry, characterized by intermittently elevated plasma lactate ranging from 3.2–4.6 mmol/L (reference 0.5–2.2) and mild, recurrent elevation of alanine aminotransferase to 68–92 U/L (reference <45), with repeat measurements on separate occasions confirming persistence of these abnormalities rather than a single isolated result. Family history is contributory: a maternal uncle is reported to have longstanding balance problems with a similar unsteady gait, and a paternal first cousin has documented recurrent unexplained elevations in lactate on prior bloodwork. Parents deny known consanguinity.

## Clinical Information
Declan Grafton is a 3-year-old male with history notable for a disorder of mitochondrial metabolism and persistent problems with coordination consistent with ataxia, impacting balance and fine motor tasks. Parents describe an unsteady, wide-based gait with frequent stumbles and difficulty with coordinated hand use during play, with symptoms present over time rather than episodic. Dysmorphic features are described on exam as mild midface hypoplasia and low-set ears. Metabolic evaluation has shown recurrent elevations in plasma lactate (typically 3.5–5.2 mmol/L on multiple draws) with an increased lactate-to-pyruvate ratio, supporting ongoing mitochondrial metabolic dysfunction; abnormalities have been noted on repeat testing rather than as a single isolated result. Family history is significant for a maternal uncle with longstanding gait imbalance described as “ataxia” beginning in childhood and a paternal first cousin with a diagnosed mitochondrial metabolism disorder; the parents deny consanguinity. Prior genetic testing included a single-gene analysis performed on 2026-01-09, which was reported as negative.

## Clinical Information
Jocelyn Claremont is an 8-year-old female with a history notable for multiple congenital anomalies, including a congenital malformation of the eyelid with persistent eyelid asymmetry affecting ocular appearance, and a congenital malformation of the external ear with atypical auricular shape since birth. She also has esophageal atresia, with neonatal feeding difficulty requiring early surgical management per outside records and ongoing need for careful eating pace and texture awareness to avoid discomfort. Developmental history includes a speech and language disorder with delayed expressive language milestones and continued difficulty with age-appropriate articulation and sentence complexity, impacting classroom communication. Dysmorphic features on exam include mild facial asymmetry and low-set, dysplastic auricles. Her constellation of congenital findings has been described in the chart as consistent with a congenital malformation syndrome predominantly affecting facial appearance, without extending beyond the specific anomalies documented above. Prior genetic evaluation included single-gene testing performed on 2025-09-29, reported as negative.

Eliana Darnell is a 6-year-old female with history notable for a disorder of aromatic amino-acid metabolism and episodic seizure-like events. Caregivers describe intermittent spells concerning for seizures without a consistently defined semiology, occurring unpredictably and prompting urgent evaluations; between episodes she returns to baseline without persistent focal deficits reported. Metabolic monitoring has demonstrated persistently elevated plasma phenylalanine with dietary sensitivity, with recent values in the 720–880 µmol/L range on multiple draws, and a mildly increased phenylalanine:tyrosine ratio; tyrosine has been low-normal at times. Parents deny known consanguinity.

Brooks North is a 10-year-old female with a history notable for multiple congenital anomalies and neurodevelopmental impairment. Records describe esophageal atresia identified in infancy, requiring early surgical management and ongoing feeding and swallowing vulnerability, with intermittent difficulty advancing textures. Ophthalmologic history includes congenital eyelid malformation consistent with blepharophimosis, with a persistently narrow palpebral fissure and reduced eyelid opening on exam. Renal history is significant for congenital polycystic kidney disease, followed longitudinally for enlarged, cystic kidneys and risk of reduced renal concentrating ability; no acute decompensation is documented in today’s referral materials. - Specimen: Blood

## Clinical Information
Brooks Iverson is a 9-year-old female with a history notable for congenital anomalies and significant neurodevelopmental impairment. Neuroimaging has documented agenesis of the corpus callosum, with longstanding global developmental delays and severe intellectual disability; she requires extensive assistance with communication, learning, and daily self-care, and has limited adaptive functioning compared with peers. Physical exam history is notable for dysmorphic craniofacial features including a flattened midface and a small lower jaw. She also has plagiocephaly, described as persistent cranial asymmetry since infancy. Cardiac history includes a secundum atrial septal defect, followed clinically as a congenital heart defect without additional detail provided here. The overall phenotype has been described as consistent with a congenital malformation syndrome predominantly affecting facial appearance, and her multisystem findings have prompted genetic evaluation. Prior testing includes a gene panel performed on 2025-12-01, which was reported as negative.

Clinical Info: Monica Rodriguez is a 10-year-old female with history notable for congenital hypotonia, described since infancy as low axial tone with “floppiness,” delayed head control, and persistent difficulty with prolonged sitting posture and stair climbing compared with peers; symptoms have been stable without a clear episodic pattern and continue to affect endurance for age-appropriate activities. She also carries a diagnosis of congenital lactase deficiency with reproducible gastrointestinal intolerance to lactose-containing foods. Caregivers report that even small amounts of milk or ice cream trigger watery diarrhea and crampy abdominal pain within a few hours, with improvement when lactose is strictly avoided. Prior metabolic evaluation has documented low small-bowel lactase activity (0.5 U/g; reference ≥10 U/g) and repeated low lactose tolerance testing with minimal rise in serum glucose after lactose load (increase 8 mg/dL), consistent with a persistent enzymatic deficiency rather than intermittent intolerance. Family history is significant for an older brother with similar lifelong lactose-triggered diarrhea requiring strict dietary avoidance, and a maternal aunt reported to have had low muscle tone in infancy requiring physical therapy. Parents deny consanguinity.

## Lab Information
- Name: Ambry
- NPI: 7167966816
- Address: 1089 Highland Ave, New Britain, CT 06230

## Test Information
- Test Type: WES
- Configuration: Duo
- Urgency: Regular
- Specimen: Blood
- Collection Date: 2026-01-20

## Clinical Information
Jennifer Evans is a 3-year-old female with history notable for multiple congenital anomalies including a congenital deformity of the feet with abnormal positioning present since birth, esophageal atresia requiring early surgical management with ongoing feeding and swallowing vulnerability, and a congenital brain malformation characterized by absence of the corpus callosum. Developmentally, she has had persistent speech and language delay with limited expressive vocabulary for age and reduced intelligibility, impacting communication in daily activities. Dysmorphic features are noted on exam with hypertelorism and low-set ears. Her overall presentation is consistent with a recognized congenital malformation syndrome involving multiple body systems, with the above structural findings and neurodevelopmental involvement documented since infancy. Prior genetic evaluation included a gene panel performed on 2025-11-25, which was reported as negative.

- Address: 4466 Pine St, West Hartford, CT 06140

## Lab Details
- Name: GeneDx
- NPI: 6627345781
- Address: 7062 Court St, Milford, CT 06594

## Test Details
- Type: WGS
- Configuration: Trio
- Urgency: Expedited
- Specimen: Blood
- Collection Date: 2026-02-12

## Clinical History
Christine Garcia is a 15-year-old female with a history notable for multiple congenital anomalies and neurodevelopmental impairment. Cardiac history includes congenital pulmonary valve atresia, with early cyanosis and exertional limitation reported in childhood and ongoing need for cardiology surveillance. Craniofacial findings include microtia, described by family as a small, malformed external ear, contributing to cosmetic concerns and difficulty with ear-level devices. Gastrointestinal history is significant for congenital esophageal atresia, repaired in infancy, with persistent feeding-related symptoms including slow eating and intermittent dysphagia to solids. Neurodevelopmental course is consistent with severe intellectual disability, with substantial limitations in learning and adaptive functioning; she requires close supervision for daily activities and attends a structured special education program. Dysmorphic features are described as short palpebral fissures and a smooth philtrum, documented on prior examinations, in the context of a congenital malformation syndrome. Prior genetic evaluation included a gene panel performed on 2025-12-04 that was negative.

## Clinical Information
- Summary: Cynthia Stewart is a 3-year-old female with a history notable for congenital brain malformation consistent with holoprosencephaly, accompanied by severe intellectual disability with global functional limitations in communication and adaptive skills. Since infancy she has had syndromic features consistent with a congenital malformation syndrome, and on exam she is described as having mild dysmorphic facial characteristics including midface hypoplasia and closely spaced eyes. Orthopedic history includes a congenital foot deformity affecting the shape and positioning of the foot, present since birth and contributing to abnormal stance and gait mechanics during supported standing and walking. - Prior Genetic Testing: chromosomal microarray (CMA) performed on 2025-12-27 was negative.

- Specimen: Blood

## Clinical Information
Aiden Kendrick is a 13-year-old male with history notable for epilepsy characterized by recurrent generalized seizures. Episodes have been described as sudden loss of awareness with diffuse stiffening and rhythmic shaking, followed by postictal sleepiness and confusion; events have occurred intermittently over time with functional impact including missed school days and activity restrictions for safety. His record also documents metabolic acidosis, with prior laboratory findings consistent with a low serum bicarbonate in the 15–18 mmol/L range and an elevated anion gap around 18–22, noted on more than one occasion rather than a single isolated draw; during these periods he has had fatigue and decreased exercise tolerance. Family history is significant for seizures in a paternal uncle and a maternal cousin. The parents are first cousins, and the family reports no other specific neurologic diagnoses beyond the seizure history noted above.

# Clinical Information
Bennett Fletcher is a 12-year-old male with history notable for phenylketonuria and epilepsy characterized by generalized seizures that are not described as intractable and without documented status epilepticus. Metabolic monitoring has shown persistently elevated plasma phenylalanine levels despite dietary sensitivity, with recent values such as phenylalanine 780 µmol/L (ref 30–120) and an elevated phenylalanine:tyrosine ratio, with tyrosine 45 µmol/L; elevations have been chronic rather than episodic per available records. Neurologically, seizures have occurred intermittently with generalized features, with events reported as recurrent rather than isolated, contributing to functional disruption due to the need for ongoing seizure precautions and recovery time after episodes. Family history is significant for a sibling with phenylketonuria and a maternal uncle with a seizure disorder, and the parents report consanguinity, specifically that they are first cousins. No prior genetic test results were provided in the available documentation for review today.

## Clinical Information
Milo Hadley is an 8-year-old male with a history notable for congenital esophageal atresia requiring early surgical management, with ongoing feeding vulnerability characterized by slow eating and intermittent dysphagia for textured solids. He also carries a diagnosis of congenital pulmonary valve atresia, followed for cyanotic congenital heart disease physiology with limited exercise tolerance compared with peers and need for close cardiology surveillance. Renal history includes polycystic kidney disease, described as bilateral renal cystic changes on prior imaging with periodic monitoring of renal function and blood pressure. Neurodevelopmentally, he has mild intellectual disability with learning difficulties that affect school performance, requiring structured educational supports and assistance with adaptive skills. Dysmorphic features consistent with a congenital malformation syndrome are described on exam as a broad nasal bridge and low-set ears. Prior genetic evaluation included chromosomal microarray testing performed on 2025-10-19, which was negative.

Clinical Information: Isla Underwood is a 13-year-old female with a complex congenital and neurodevelopmental history. Records document an atrial septal defect, with ongoing cardiology follow-up noted in prior documentation. Neuroimaging history is notable for a congenital brain malformation described as agenesis of the corpus callosum. Developmentally, she has longstanding global cognitive impairment consistent with severe intellectual disability, requiring substantial support for learning and daily adaptive skills. Dysmorphic features are present, including a triangular facial shape and low-set ears. Craniofacial history also includes plagiocephaly, described as persistent head asymmetry since infancy. A syndromic diagnosis consistent with a named congenital malformation syndrome is carried in her chart, in the setting of the above structural and developmental findings, without additional organ-specific details available in the current note. Prior genetic evaluation included single-gene testing performed on 2025-09-25, which was reported as negative.

- Test Type: WES
- Configuration: Duo
- Urgency: Expedited
- Specimen: Buccal
- Collection Date: N/A

## Clinical Information
Atticus Valentine is an 11-year-old male with a history notable for congenital microtia, congenital deformity of the feet, and dysmorphic features consistent with a congenital malformation syndrome. Parents report that the external ear difference has been present since birth and has been associated with cosmetic asymmetry; exam is notable for a small, underdeveloped auricle. He also has a structural foot deformity noted since infancy, with persistent abnormal foot positioning that affects shoe fit and contributes to clumsiness with higher-impact activities. Developmental history is significant for a specific developmental disorder of motor function, with longstanding difficulty in fine and gross motor coordination; caregivers describe slower acquisition of skills such as handwriting, buttoning, and age-appropriate sports participation, with fatigue and frustration during tasks requiring sustained coordination. Dysmorphic features on exam include mild facial asymmetry and low-set ears. Prior genetic testing included chromosomal microarray performed on 2025-12-06, which was negative.

**Issue:** The requested test is **Whole Exome Sequencing (WES) Trio (Expedited)**, but the patient's clinical notes in the EHR describe acute trauma and environmental exposures: a **concussion**, a **superficial facial burn**, and an **accidental antibiotic ingestion**.

In accordance with your instructions to 'immediately stop the process and report the issue' if any were found, I have terminated the form submission.

There is a positive family history of similar lactose intolerance in the mother and a maternal uncle, and seizures in a paternal cousin during childhood. Parents deny consanguinity.

## Clinical Information
Jasper Prescott is a 9-year-old female with a history notable for brain malformation consistent with congenital absence of the corpus callosum and associated global neurodevelopmental impact, including moderate intellectual disability with ongoing needs for support in learning and adaptive skills. Craniofacial history includes plagiocephaly, and on exam she has mild dysmorphic facial features with a relatively flat nasal bridge and low-set ears. She also carries a diagnosis of Joubert syndrome, and her clinical course has included congenital neurologic involvement consistent with that condition. Renal history is significant for autosomal recessive polycystic kidney disease, described as enlarged echogenic kidneys with multiple small cysts on prior imaging and ongoing monitoring for renal complications. Family history is not documented as contributory in the available record. Prior genetic evaluation included chromosomal microarray testing performed on 2025-11-09, which was negative.

Leo Rutherford is a 5-year-old male evaluated after a head injury with an open scalp laceration and concussion without loss of consciousness. The event resulted in a visible linear scalp cut with active bleeding at the time of injury; the wound was cleaned and dressed, and there has been localized scalp tenderness since. Caregivers report he remained awake and responsive throughout the incident with no witnessed loss of consciousness, and he has been somewhat more fatigued and irritable than baseline since the injury, with reduced tolerance for screen time and play. No focal neurologic deficits are described in the available history. Family history is notable for parental consanguinity; the parents are first cousins.

- Configuration: Duo
- Urgency: Expedited
- Specimen: Saliva
- Collection Date: 2026-01-19

**Clinical Information:**
Paul Roberts is an 11-year-old male with history notable for hyperphenylalaninemia and epilepsy characterized as other generalized epilepsy with intractable seizures, without status epilepticus. Metabolic monitoring has shown persistently elevated plasma phenylalanine levels, most recently 780 µmol/L (reference 30–120) with tyrosine at the low end of normal at 35 µmol/L (reference 30–120), with prior values typically ranging 600–900 µmol/L during periods of dietary liberalization. Caregivers describe sensitivity of levels to protein intake, with episodic rises noted after higher-protein meals. Neurologically, he has recurrent generalized seizures that remain difficult to control, occurring several times per month with postictal sleepiness and missed school days; there has been no history of convulsive status epilepticus. Family history is significant for an older maternal uncle with a childhood-onset seizure disorder and a paternal cousin reported to have elevated phenylalanine detected on newborn screening; no parental consanguinity is reported.

clinical_rationale.txt:
Christine Garcia is a 15-year-old female with a history notable for multiple congenital anomalies and neurodevelopmental impairment. Cardiac history includes congenital pulmonary valve atresia, with early cyanosis and exertional limitation reported in childhood and ongoing need for cardiology surveillance. Craniofacial findings include microtia, described by family as a small, malformed external ear, contributing to cosmetic concerns and difficulty with ear-level devices. Gastrointestinal history is significant for congenital esophageal atresia, repaired in infancy, with persistent feeding-related symptoms including slow eating and intermittent dysphagia to solids. Neurodevelopmental course is consistent with severe intellectual disability, with substantial limitations in learning and adaptive functioning; she requires close supervision for daily activities and attends a structured special education program. Dysmorphic features are described as short palpebral fissures and a smooth philtrum, documented on prior examinations, in the context of a congenital malformation syndrome. Prior genetic evaluation included a gene panel performed on 2025-12-04 that was negative.

# Clinical Information
Larry Hernandez is a 3-year-old male with a history notable for multiple congenital anomalies and severe intellectual disability. Congenital eye findings include other congenital malformations of the eyelid, contributing to persistent ocular asymmetry on exam. Cardiac history includes Ebstein’s anomaly, with ongoing cardiology follow-up for a structural tricuspid valve defect. Renal history is significant for congenital polycystic kidney disease, with known bilateral cystic changes and monitoring for kidney function over time. Neurodevelopmentally, he meets criteria for severe intellectual disability, with marked limitations in adaptive functioning and significant support needs for communication and daily activities. Overall presentation is consistent with a congenital malformation syndrome predominantly affecting facial appearance and the musculoskeletal system, and on exam he has dysmorphic facial features including downslanting palpebral fissures and a flat nasal bridge. Prior genetic testing is documented: chromosomal microarray performed on 2025-08-23 was negative.

- Urgency: Regular
- Specimen: Buccal
- Collection Date: 2025-12-12

## Clinical Info
Patricia Hall is a 13-year-old female with history notable for multiple congenital anomalies and developmental delay. Congenital findings include a congenital deformity of the foot present since birth, with persistent structural malalignment affecting shoe fit and gait mechanics. She also has congenital polycystic kidney disease, described as bilateral renal cystic changes identified in childhood, followed clinically for renal involvement. Craniofacial morphology is notable for a small jaw consistent with mandibular hypoplasia, and she has mild dysmorphic features on exam characterized by micrognathia and a flattened midface profile. Neurodevelopmental history includes global developmental delay with a prominent speech and language disorder; caregivers report ongoing difficulty with expressive language and articulation that interferes with classroom participation and peer communication. Her overall presentation is consistent with a defined congenital malformation syndrome involving multiple systems, without extending beyond the specific features documented above. Prior genetic evaluation included a gene panel performed on 2025-12-16, which was reported as negative.

# Clinical Information
Maria Nelson is a 9-year-old female with a history notable for congenital anomalies and developmental motor delay. She has been followed for blepharophimosis with a narrowed palpebral fissure and associated eyelid narrowing noted since infancy. She also has a congenital esophageal atresia, with early feeding difficulties and need for surgical repair in infancy per outside records, and she continues to have intermittent dysphagia with certain textures. Neurodevelopmental history includes abnormalities of the corpus callosum identified on prior neuroimaging, with associated functional impact in coordination and motor planning. She carries a diagnosis of specific developmental disorder of motor function, described by caregivers as persistent fine and gross motor delays affecting handwriting, dressing tasks, and participation in age-appropriate sports despite supportive therapies. Dysmorphic features documented on exam include facial asymmetry and mild micrognathia. Her presentation has been considered within the scope of a congenital malformation syndrome predominantly affecting facial appearance. Prior genetic testing includes chromosomal microarray performed on 2025-12-03, reported as negative.

## Test Information
- Test Type: WES
- Configuration: Proband
- Urgency: Regular
- Specimen: Saliva
- Collection Date: 2025-12-19

## Clinical Information
Cynthia Stewart is a 3-year-old female with a history notable for congenital brain malformation consistent with holoprosencephaly, accompanied by severe intellectual disability with global functional limitations in communication and adaptive skills. Since infancy she has had syndromic features consistent with a congenital malformation syndrome, and on exam she is described as having mild dysmorphic facial characteristics including midface hypoplasia and closely spaced eyes. Orthopedic history includes a congenital foot deformity affecting the shape and positioning of the foot, present since birth and contributing to abnormal stance and gait mechanics during supported standing and walking. Developmentally, caregivers describe very limited acquisition of age-expected cognitive and self-care milestones, requiring extensive assistance with daily activities and close supervision for safety. Prior genetic testing is documented: chromosomal microarray (CMA) performed on 2025-12-27 was negative.

## Test Information
- Test Type: WES
- Configuration: Trio
- Urgency: Expedited
- Specimen: Blood
- Collection Date: 2026-02-10

## Clinical Information
- Notes: Asher Osborn is an 8-year-old male with history notable for an inborn error of phenylalanine metabolism consistent with a non-classical hyperphenylalaninemia phenotype. Available laboratory data show persistent elevation of plasma phenylalanine on serial monitoring, typically ranging 420–680 µmol/L with relatively stable tyrosine around 55–75 µmol/L, with reported dietary sensitivity such that higher natural protein intake is followed by higher phenylalanine values on repeat testing. Neurologically, he has longstanding gait and coordination difficulty described as truncal unsteadiness with frequent stumbling, difficulty with tandem walking, and impaired balance on stairs; symptoms are present daily and interfere with running and age-appropriate sports participation. Family history is significant for a paternal uncle with a similar disorder of phenylalanine metabolism diagnosed in childhood and managed with dietary modification, and a maternal first cousin with chronic coordination problems characterized by an unsteady gait. The parents report consanguinity, specifically that they are first cousins.

Clinical Indications: Isla Underwood is a 13-year-old female with a complex congenital and neurodevelopmental history. Records document an atrial septal defect, with ongoing cardiology follow-up noted in prior documentation. Neuroimaging history is notable for a congenital brain malformation described as agenesis of the corpus callosum. Developmentally, she has longstanding global cognitive impairment consistent with severe intellectual disability, requiring substantial support for learning and daily adaptive skills. Dysmorphic features are present, including a triangular facial shape and low-set ears. Craniofacial history also includes plagiocephaly, described as persistent head asymmetry since infancy. A syndromic diagnosis consistent with a named congenital malformation syndrome is carried in her chart, in the setting of the above structural and developmental findings, without additional organ-specific details available in the current note. Prior genetic evaluation included single-gene testing performed on 2025-09-25, which was reported as negative.

- Name: GeneDx
- NPI: 1687231732
- Address: 3303 Court St, Manchester, CT 06371

## Test Information
- Test Type: WES
- Configuration: Trio
- Urgency: Regular
- Specimen: Saliva
- Collection Date: 2026-02-17

## Clinical Information
Skye Alder is a 6-year-old female with history notable since infancy for congenital hypotonia, described by caregivers as low muscle tone with poor head control early on and persistent core weakness that affects endurance with age-appropriate gross motor activities. She has had laboratory findings of other specified abnormal blood chemistry, with episodic elevations in serum lactate (reported up to 3.8 mmol/L, ref 0.5–2.2) and mild, persistent elevation of plasma ammonia (typically 65–80 µmol/L, ref <50) on repeat checks; caregivers note these abnormalities are sometimes more pronounced during intercurrent illness but have also been present when she is otherwise at baseline. Family history is significant for consanguinity, with the parents being first cousins. There is also a reported history of low muscle tone in a maternal uncle in childhood, without a clearly documented formal diagnosis, and a paternal cousin with recurrent abnormal blood chemistry findings noted during pediatric evaluations. No prior genetic test results were provided in the available record for review.

Secondary medical issues include a recent scalp laceration requiring acute care and a concussion without loss of consciousness after a head injury; family reports brief post-injury headache and transient irritability with return to baseline behavior and activity thereafter.

No seizures are described in the available history. Family history is significant for an older brother with a similar lifelong unsteady gait and need for school-based motor supports, and a maternal uncle reported to have chronic balance problems beginning in childhood. Parents deny consanguinity. Secondary medical issues include a recent scalp laceration treated as an initial encounter after minor trauma, an accidental penicillin exposure consistent with an unintentional antibiotic poisoning event, and a right ankle sprain involving unspecified ligament(s) managed as an initial encounter."

**Clinical Information:**
Tristan Emerson is a 12-year-old male with history notable for neonatal hypotonia described as diminished spontaneous movement and poor flexor tone in the newborn period, with early motor delay requiring supportive therapies. Ongoing neurologic history is characterized by low muscle tone and easy fatigability with prolonged activity, impacting endurance for age-appropriate play and sports, without documentation here of a specific neuromuscular diagnosis. Metabolic evaluation has been pursued for persistently abnormal blood chemistry findings; available results include recurrent mild elevations of plasma lactate (typically 2.8–3.6 mmol/L, ref 0.5–2.2) and intermittent elevation of ammonia (up to 78 µmol/L, ref 11–35) on repeat sampling, without additional named inborn error specified in the record. Family history is significant for consanguinity, with the parents reported to be first cousins. There is also a history of a similarly affected sibling with neonatal hypotonia and delayed motor milestones, and a maternal uncle with lifelong low muscle tone and exercise intolerance. No prior genetic test type, date, or result information is provided in the available documentation.

Secondary medical issues include an acute superficial burn of an unspecified site without stated depth, a documented episode of accidental penicillin ingestion, and a right ankle sprain involving an unspecified ligament after an injury, managed as an acute event without chronic sequelae described here.

I have encountered a significant issue and am stopping the Pre-Authorization process for Milo Marlow as requested.

The patient record for Milo Marlow (Member ID: CT270727692) indicates the clinical reasons for the visit are an acute burn to the head and face and an accidental exposure to penicillin. These are accidental injuries and acute toxic effects, not genetic conditions. A request for Whole Genome Sequencing (WGS) is clinically inappropriate for these indications.

I have encountered a significant issue and, as per your instructions, have stopped the pre-authorization process for Tristan Irwin. 

**Issue Identified:**
There is a critical mismatch between the requested test and the patient's clinical indications. The provider has requested a **WES Duo (Whole Exome Sequencing)**, which is a genetic test, but the patient's EHR record indicates he is being treated for **acute trauma** (concussion without loss of consciousness, headache, mental fog, and superficial burns from a recent head injury). Furthermore, the EHR explicitly states: 'No family history or prior genetic/metabolic testing information is available in the record for this encounter.'

**Summary of Progress Before Stopping:**
- **Logged in:** Successfully as 'user2'.
- **Patient Identified:** Tristan Irwin (Member ID: HUS786779605, DOB: 2012-02-03).
- **Sections Completed:**

    3. **Test Requested:** Filled (WES Duo, Expedited, Specimen: Saliva, CPT: 81415, 81416, Internal Code: 80002).

Because genetic testing is not typically indicated for acute head injuries and burns, I have halted the submission to prevent a likely denial and to report this discrepancy.

Prior genetic evaluation included a gene panel performed on 2025-09-22, which was reported as negative."}

## Clinical Information
Ezra Alder is an 11-year-old male with history notable for epilepsy characterized by generalized seizures that have been described as difficult to fully control, with breakthrough events occurring intermittently despite ongoing neurologic follow-up. Episodes are reported as sudden loss of awareness with generalized convulsive activity and postictal fatigue, with functional impact including missed school days and need for close supervision during higher-risk activities. His medical course is also notable for metabolic acidosis, documented on multiple occasions with low serum bicarbonate and increased anion gap; recent representative chemistry showed CO₂ 16 mmol/L (ref 22–29) with anion gap 18, with similar values noted on repeat testing, suggesting a persistent tendency rather than a single isolated episode. Symptoms during more pronounced acidosis have included fatigue and reduced exercise tolerance, without additional diagnostic labels assigned beyond the documented acid–base disturbance. Family history is significant for seizures in a paternal uncle and recurrent “low bicarbonate” episodes in a maternal aunt. The parents are first cousins, and there is no additional pedigree information available in the current record.

- Name: Ambry
- NPI: 2491030900
- Address: 9050 Sunset Ave, Milford, CT 06039

## Test Information
- Test Type: WES
- Configuration: Duo
- Urgency: Expedited
- Specimen: Buccal
- Collection Date: 2026-01-23

## Clinical Information
Victoria Jones is an 8-year-old female with history notable for developmental delay with moderate intellectual disability, requiring ongoing support for learning and adaptive skills. She has craniofacial and musculoskeletal findings consistent with a congenital multiple-anomaly pattern, including a congenital facial asymmetry with hemifacial underdevelopment and associated jaw/facial contour imbalance, along with a congenital chest wall configuration described as pectus carinatum. Cardiac history includes a known atrial septal defect consistent with an ostium secundum-type defect, followed clinically as a structural congenital heart finding. Dysmorphic features on exam are characterized by clear facial asymmetry and a prominent anterior chest wall contour. Prior genetic evaluation included a single-gene test performed on 2025-12-01, which was reported as negative.

  "clinical_info": "Laura Lee is a 12-year-old female with history notable for congenital lactase deficiency with lifelong lactose intolerance. Since infancy, dairy exposure has reliably triggered watery diarrhea, abdominal cramping, bloating, and excessive gas, with symptoms beginning within hours of ingestion and improving with strict lactose avoidance; she tolerates lactose-free products. Metabolic evaluation has been consistent with carbohydrate malabsorption, including a hydrogen breath test showing an early rise in breath hydrogen to 45 ppm above baseline at 60 minutes after lactose load, and stool studies during symptomatic episodes demonstrating acidic stool (pH 5.0) with positive reducing substances. Neurologic history is notable for episodes of seizure-like activity without a specified seizure type; events have been intermittent, described as abrupt staring with unresponsiveness and generalized shaking lasting 1–2 minutes, followed by post-event fatigue, with no clear provocation identified. Family history is significant for a father with lactose intolerance beginning in childhood and a maternal uncle with recurrent seizures treated with antiseizure medication."
}

## Test
- Type: WES
- Configuration: Proband
- Urgency: Expedited
- Specimen: Buccal
- Collection Date: 2026-02-12

## Clinical Info
Jennifer Wright is a 6-year-old female with history notable for congenital lactase deficiency with lifelong intolerance to lactose-containing foods. Since infancy, exposure to breast milk/formula or later dairy products has reliably triggered profuse watery diarrhea, abdominal cramping, bloating, and irritability, with improvement on strict lactose avoidance; even small amounts of hidden lactose (e.g., in processed foods) can provoke symptoms within hours. Metabolic evaluation has shown persistent carbohydrate malabsorption consistent with lactase deficiency, including repeated stool testing with acidic pH around 5.0 and positive reducing substances (1–2+), and breath hydrogen testing demonstrating a marked rise after lactose challenge (baseline 8 ppm to 62 ppm at 90 minutes). Neurologic history includes ataxia manifested by an unsteady, wide-based gait and poor coordination, with frequent tripping and difficulty with tandem walking and stairs, affecting playground activities; symptoms are described as chronic rather than episodic. Family history is notable for lactose intolerance in the father and a paternal aunt with longstanding dairy-triggered diarrhea; parents deny consanguinity.

# Patient Information: Naomi Easton

- **Relation:** Guardian

## Provider Information
- **Name:** Daniel Jackson
- **NPI:** 7772930700
- **Phone:** (860) 286-2518
- **Fax:** (860) 824-7213
- **Address:** 814 Washington Ave, Shelton, CT 06461

## Lab Information
- **Name:** Invitae
- **NPI:** 6138509296
- **Address:** 2561 Washington Ave, Enfield, CT 06276

## Test Information
- **Test Type:** WES
- **Configuration:** Trio
- **Urgency:** Regular
- **Specimen:** Saliva
- **Collection Date:** 2026-02-18

## Clinical Information
Naomi Easton is a 7-year-old female with history notable for a disorder of mitochondrial metabolism and intermittent neonatal hypotonia. Per caregiver report, low muscle tone was first recognized in the newborn period with “floppiness” and reduced spontaneous antigravity movements, requiring close monitoring early in life; tone improved over time but she continues to fatigue more quickly than peers with prolonged activity. Metabolic evaluation has been consistent with a mitochondrial metabolic disorder, with episodic biochemical decompensation during intercurrent illnesses characterized by elevated serum lactate (typically 3.5–5.2 mmol/L; baseline ~1.8–2.2 mmol/L) and increased lactate-to-pyruvate ratio (reported 25–32 during episodes), with partial normalization when well. Caregivers describe that episodes are associated with decreased stamina and need for frequent rest, but without additional neurologic focality described in today’s history. Family history is significant for a maternal aunt with a diagnosed mitochondrial metabolic disorder and exercise intolerance, and a maternal cousin with early hypotonia in infancy; parents deny consanguinity. No prior genetic test results were provided in the available record for review today.

Clinical Info: Daphne Lennox is a 7-year-old female with history notable for congenital lactase deficiency with lifelong lactose intolerance. Since infancy, dairy exposure has reliably triggered profuse watery diarrhea, abdominal cramping, and bloating within hours of ingestion, with improvement when lactose is strictly avoided; symptoms recur with accidental exposures and have limited dietary flexibility at school and social events. Metabolic evaluation has shown persistent evidence of carbohydrate malabsorption, including repeatedly elevated stool reducing substances (2+) and acidic stool pH around 5.0 during symptomatic periods, with otherwise stable basic chemistries and no dehydration at recent checks. Neurologic history includes episodes of seizure-like activity without a specified epilepsy syndrome. Events have been intermittent and described as sudden unresponsiveness with generalized stiffening and rhythmic shaking lasting approximately 1–2 minutes, followed by post-event sleepiness; frequency has varied from isolated occurrences to clusters separated by months. Family history is significant for a father with lactose intolerance since childhood and a maternal aunt with recurrent seizures beginning in adolescence; there is no known consanguinity reported.

- Clinical Information: Callum Westley is a 7-year-old male with history notable for gait and coordination difficulties consistent with ataxia. Caregivers describe an unsteady, wide-based walk with frequent stumbling, worse when tired and on uneven surfaces, and difficulty with rapid alternating movements and balance-dependent tasks such as climbing stairs or running with peers. Metabolic evaluation has identified persistent, nonspecific abnormalities on blood chemistry testing, documented on repeat measurements rather than a single episode. No consanguinity is reported.

The parents are first cousins, and there is no additional pedigree detail available in the record for other affected relatives."

# Patient Information: Nolan Kendrick

- **Name:** Nolan Kendrick

## Subscriber Information

## Provider Information

- **Fax:** (959) 967-1805
- **Address:** 9955 Pine St, New Haven, CT 06156

## Lab Information
- **Name:** Ambry
- **NPI:** 7103671782
- **Address:** 3491 Meadow Ln, Glastonbury, CT 06905

## Test Information
- **Test Type:** WES
- **Configuration:** Trio
- **Urgency:** Regular
- **Specimen:** Blood
- **Collection Date:** 2026-02-11

## Clinical Information
Nolan Kendrick is a 9-year-old male with history notable for a disorder of mitochondrial metabolism and epilepsy characterized by generalized seizures that are not intractable and without documented status epilepticus. Seizure history includes recurrent generalized events with postictal sleepiness and missed school days after breakthrough episodes. Metabolic evaluation has shown a pattern consistent with mitochondrial dysfunction, including persistently elevated lactate on multiple draws (typically 3.6–4.8 mmol/L, ref 0.5–2.2) with an increased lactate-to-pyruvate ratio around 28–32, and intermittent mild elevation of plasma alanine (e.g., 620 µmol/L, ref 200–550). Abnormalities have been present across repeated outpatient testing rather than a single acute illness-only finding. Family history is significant for a maternal uncle with a seizure disorder beginning in childhood and a maternal grandmother reported to have longstanding exercise intolerance and episodic fatigue; there is no reported parental consanguinity. Overall functional impact is driven by episodic seizures and fluctuating endurance, with fatigue more prominent after intercurrent infections or prolonged exertion.

- Specimen: Saliva

## Clinical Information
Arlo Quarry is a 14-year-old male with history notable for a congenital hypotonia syndrome, with low muscle tone recognized since infancy and ongoing functional impact including poor postural endurance and delayed acquisition of gross motor skills compared with peers. On exam today he appears dysmorphic with a long, narrow face and low-set ears. He also carries a history of metabolic acidosis. Prior records and interval labs are consistent with a persistent, non–anion gap metabolic acidosis, with serum bicarbonate typically 15–18 mmol/L (most recently 16 mmol/L) and corresponding mild acidemia (venous pH around 7.30), contributing to episodic fatigue and reduced exercise tolerance. Family history is significant for similarly affected relatives, including an older brother with lifelong low muscle tone and documented low serum bicarbonate values in adolescence, and a paternal aunt reported to have recurrent episodes of “acidosis” requiring medical evaluation. The parents are first cousins. Prior genetic testing included a gene panel performed on 2025-12-19, reported as negative.

Congenital findings include a documented congenital deformity of the foot with persistent structural asymmetry affecting shoe fit and gait mechanics. He also carries a diagnosis of congenital renal cystic disease, described as cystic involvement of the kidneys identified in childhood and followed clinically as a structural renal condition. Ocular history includes a congenital malformation of the eyelid; exam descriptions in the record note an abnormal eyelid contour present since birth, contributing to visible facial asymmetry. Overall phenotype is consistent with a recognized congenital malformation syndrome involving multiple systems. Prior genetic evaluation included single-gene testing performed on 2025-12-23, reported as negative.

Metabolic monitoring has shown a persistent pattern of elevated lactate, with venous lactate typically around 3.8–4.6 mmol/L (ref 0.5–2.2) on multiple checks, and intermittent elevation during intercurrent illness, consistent with episodic metabolic decompensation; bicarbonate has at times been mildly reduced (18–20 mmol/L) during these episodes with associated lethargy and reduced exercise tolerance. No consanguinity is reported.

Clinical Info: Skye Alder is a 6-year-old female with history notable since infancy for congenital hypotonia, described by caregivers as low muscle tone with poor head control early on and persistent core weakness that affects endurance with age-appropriate gross motor activities. She has had laboratory findings of other specified abnormal blood chemistry, with episodic elevations in serum lactate (reported up to 3.8 mmol/L, ref 0.5–2.2) and mild, persistent elevation of plasma ammonia (typically 65–80 µmol/L, ref <50) on repeat checks; caregivers note these abnormalities are sometimes more pronounced during intercurrent illness but have also been present when she is otherwise at baseline. Family history is significant for consanguinity, with the parents being first cousins. There is also a reported history of low muscle tone in a maternal uncle in childhood, without a clearly documented formal diagnosis, and a paternal cousin with recurrent abnormal blood chemistry findings noted during pediatric evaluations. No prior genetic test results were provided in the available record for review.

Secondary medical issues include a recent scalp laceration requiring acute care and a concussion without loss of consciousness after a head injury; family reports brief post-injury headache and transient irritability with return to baseline behavior and activity thereafter.

## Test Information
- Test Type: WES
- Configuration: Trio
- Urgency: Regular
- Specimen: Blood
- Collection Date: N/A

## Clinical Information
Naomi Dalton is a 15-year-old female with history notable for episodic metabolic acidosis and impaired coordination. Episodes have been characterized by low serum bicarbonate with acidemia on blood gas; during a recent symptomatic period, venous pH was 7.28 with bicarbonate 14 mmol/L and an elevated anion gap of 20, with partial improvement after supportive care and interval recurrence reported on separate occasions, suggesting a relapsing pattern rather than a single isolated event. During these periods she and her family also describe unsteady gait and clumsiness, with difficulty sustaining tandem walking and needing to slow down on stairs; symptoms fluctuate and can interfere with school activities requiring balance and fine motor control. Family history is significant for similar episodes of “acid build-up” requiring emergency evaluation in a maternal uncle, and a sibling with longstanding coordination difficulties affecting sports participation. The parents are first cousins, and the family reports multiple relatives from the same lineage with intermittent balance problems.

# Patient Details: Soren Kingsley

- **Sex**: Male

- **Address**: 3594 Second St, Torrington, CT 06972

## Subscriber Information

- **Phone**: (475) 963-2677
- **Fax**: (475) 578-6816
- **Address**: 3150 Water St, Torrington, CT 06758

## Lab Information
- **Name**: LabCorp
- **NPI**: 9325396318
- **Address**: 3298 Hill St, Shelton, CT 06076

## Test Information
- **Test Type**: WES
- **Configuration**: Trio
- **Urgency**: Expedited
- **Specimen**: Blood
- **Collection Date**: 2026-02-08

## Clinical Information
- **Summary**: Soren Kingsley is a 6-year-old male with history notable for congenital lactase deficiency with lifelong lactose intolerance. Caregivers report that ingestion of lactose-containing foods reliably triggers watery diarrhea, abdominal cramping, and excessive gas within a few hours; symptoms improve with strict avoidance and recur with accidental exposures at school or social events. Metabolic evaluation has shown persistently low lactase activity consistent with the diagnosis, and supportive stool testing during symptomatic periods has demonstrated acidic stool (pH ~5.0) with positive reducing substances. Neurologic history is notable for ataxia with an unsteady, wide-based gait and impaired coordination, including difficulty with tandem walking and frequent stumbling on uneven surfaces; this has impacted participation in age-appropriate play and running. Family history is significant for lactose intolerance due to congenital lactase deficiency in a paternal uncle and a similarly affected older sibling; the parents are first cousins. Prior records include a genetic/metabolic workup dated 2023-06-14 documenting congenital lactase deficiency.

Secondary medical issues include a recent accidental penicillin exposure resulting in poisoning by penicillins, treated as an acute event, and a scalp laceration without foreign body that required acute care; both are documented as initial encounters.

On exam, coordination is impaired with an ataxic gait pattern, without describing a specific cerebellar syndrome beyond observed unsteadiness. Metabolic evaluation has shown persistent elevation of venous lactate, most recently 3.8 mmol/L (ref 0.5–2.2) with an elevated lactate-to-pyruvate ratio of 28 (ref 10–20), and intermittent mild alanine elevation (564 µmol/L, ref 200–500), supporting a chronic metabolic pattern rather than an isolated episode. Family history is significant for parental consanguinity; the parents are first cousins. A maternal uncle is reported to have a similar lifelong unsteady gait and exercise intolerance, and a paternal cousin has a diagnosed mitochondrial metabolism disorder with episodic fatigue and poor stamina."
}
